jonathan-anderson-s-dior-redefines-herit.title: Jonathan Anderson's Dior Redefines Heritage
jonathan-anderson-s-dior-redefines-herit.author: Willie Austin
jonathan-anderson-s-dior-redefines-herit.dek: In his sophomore ready-to-wear outing, the Northern Irish designer transforms the Tuileries into a Monet and the Bar jacket into something you might actually want to wear.

body.text: A massive glass greenhouse rose from the Jardin des Tuileries on the afternoon of March 3, enclosing the park's octagonal basin in golden light. The water had been dotted with artificial lily pads — a direct invocation of Claude Monet that felt less like set decoration and more like a thesis statement. Jonathan Anderson's second ready-to-wear collection for Christian Dior was about seeing and being seen, about the promenade as performance, about Louis XIV's pleasure gardens and Charles Baudelaire's 1857 poem 'A une passante.' It was also, more simply, about making very beautiful clothes.

The 66-look collection confirmed what Anderson's debut had only hinted at: he has a clear, confident vision for Dior that is softer, stranger, and more deeply rooted in craft than anything the house has shown in years. Where his predecessor Maria Grazia Chiuri often foregrounded feminist messaging, Anderson foregrounds the garment itself — its construction, its texture, the way light moves across fabric.
Deconstructing the Bar

The Bar jacket — Christian Dior's signature from 1947 — appeared throughout the collection, but never as a museum piece. Anderson reimagined it as a soft gray knitted cardigan with a scrolling peplum, then as a crinkled fabric version that functioned somewhere between a cardigan and a jacket. Some were gently elongated, with waists hinted at rather than sharply cinched. Others were cropped and puffed, stuffed with what looked like petticoats beneath — ready-to-wear quoting menswear, which had itself reworked Dior's original New Look archetype.

This kind of recursive referencing is Anderson's great talent. He doesn't simply revisit the archive; he puts it through a hall of mirrors. 'These brands are institutions,' he told interviewer Bella Freud in a sit-down before the show. 'And you're trying to break down these institutions, ultimately. Dior can get very heavy in history. It has this giant past, but it has to release itself.'
Materials and Mastery

The material range was staggering. Chantilly lace, metallic jacquards, dotted Swiss fabric, hammered silk, Japanese embroidered denim, shearling, and sculptural knits — all appeared within the first twenty looks. A pair of ivory hammered silk track pants with covered bridal buttons was described backstage as a new 'entry point' into the brand, a nod to Anderson's understanding that Dior must sell to real people, not just dress mannequins in museums.

The standout pieces told their own stories. Dotted Swiss ruffle skirts with long trains offered a youthful take on Christian Dior's legendary Junon gown. Jeans with ribbon embroidery were, in Anderson's words, 'sort of high-low, but in a good way.' Plain robe coats worn as dresses had the effortless quality of something thrown on at midnight and still looking magnificent at dawn.
The Details That Matter

Anderson, who was born in Magherafelt, Northern Ireland in 1984 and once studied acting at Juilliard before pivoting to fashion, brings a theatrical sensibility to accessories. Water lily oversized brooches with yellow centers and fluttery pink metal petals sat on lapels. Heels were shaped into water lilies and roses. A frog-shaped clutch in green velvet and gold hardware was destined for Instagram before it even left the runway. Shoes were designed in collaboration with Nina Christen, continuing a partnership that has quietly elevated Dior's footwear.

It is worth remembering that Anderson's debut womenswear show was put together in just 26 days. That he has arrived at this level of sophistication in his sophomore season suggests a designer who knows exactly where he's going. Named WWD's 2025 Newsmaker of the Year, Anderson continues to run JW Anderson concurrently — a balancing act that seems to sharpen rather than dilute his vision.
The Verdict

The front row — Anya Taylor-Joy, Charlize Theron, Jisoo, Priyanka Chopra Jonas, Pharrell Williams, Willow Smith, Emily Ratajkowski, and Macaulay Culkin among them — watched a designer find his stride. The Washington Post headlined its review 'Dior's Jonathan Anderson finds his stride with a garden of earthly delights.' Coveteur praised him for 'playing with sentiments of the real and the unreal with ease.' The Fashion Spot called it 'exquisite.'

Anderson's Dior is not about revolution. It's about the slow, deliberate work of making an institution feel alive again — of taking something as heavy as history and making it float, like a lily pad on still water.

piccioli-s-balenciaga-clair-obscur.title: Piccioli's Balenciaga: Clair-Obscur
piccioli-s-balenciaga-clair-obscur.author: Willie Austin
piccioli-s-balenciaga-clair-obscur.dek: In a darkened former Adidas flagship on the Champs-Elysees, Pierpaolo Piccioli stages 81 looks that marry Cristobal's architecture with his own radical humanism.

body.text: The venue told you everything you needed to know before a single model appeared. A vast, darkened space on the Champs-Elysees — a former Adidas flagship store, stripped bare and carpeted in black — with towering video screens showing premiere clips from the third season of HBO's 'Euphoria,' interspersed with deserted landscapes and broiling sunsets. Filmmaker Sam Levinson, who created the series, collaborated with Pierpaolo Piccioli on the environment. Concert lighting sliced through the darkness. This was fashion as cinema, cinema as fashion.

Piccioli titled his second collection for Balenciaga 'ClairObscur' — after the French term for the Italian chiaroscuro, the High Renaissance technique of rendering light against darkness to create depth and volume. According to the collection notes: 'The centrality of and focus on the human form expanded the methodologies of Cristobal Balenciaga, with the body itself becoming the structure inside garments.'
81 Exercises in Tension

The 81 co-ed looks filed down a thin path of light. The opening sequence was almost entirely black: a leather-bomber-and-pencil-skirt set with a high front slit, a hip-length peacoat with a dramatic raised collar over leggings, a belted long-sleeve jumpsuit, a leather cape dress. They read as armor — protection against a world that Piccioli clearly finds bruising.

Then came the ruptures. Sudden jolts of phosphorescent orange, bruised purple, and deep burgundy cut through the darkness like flashes of emotion. Oversized cocoon coats hovered away from the body in midnight velvet. Sculpted gowns with built-in corsetry moved with unexpected fluidity. The closing sequence — all white, all sheer — was a masterclass in the tension between concealing and revealing.

'Right now I feel the radical act is to be human,' Piccioli said during a preview. 'The world feels harsh, and designing in a human way may be the most disruptive thing any of us can do.' Coming from a designer who spent approximately 25 years at Valentino — the house of red, of romance, of grandeur — this pivot toward darkness carries real weight.
The Euphoria Connection

The collaboration with Levinson was more than set dressing. Screens premiered footage from Euphoria's third season, set to debut April 12, and Levinson sat front row alongside Winona Ryder, Harris Dickinson, Naomi Watts, and Nick Rhodes. 'Sam is able to tell stories — not judging, not criticizing, not celebrating — but just giving a point of view that is very human and very emotional,' Piccioli explained.

One critic described the collection as 'a chiaroscuro poem rendered in leather, wool, and light — a fresco of humanity painted with silhouettes rather than brushstrokes.' Accessories reinforced the duality: the new HG Avenue bag and George bag appeared alongside crystallized futuristic sunglasses, chunky statement sneakers, and leather opera gloves. Clair-obscur effects were applied to handbags and D'Orsay sneakers, light and dark literally splitting each object in half.

This is the collection where Piccioli's Balenciaga point of view finally clicked — where Cristobal's architectural legacy stopped being a constraint and became a collaborator. Whether the market follows the vision remains to be seen, but the artistic argument is no longer in doubt.

sarah-burton-s-givenchy-finds-its-voice-.title: Sarah Burton's Givenchy Finds Its Voice in Third Outing
sarah-burton-s-givenchy-finds-its-voice-.author: Willie Austin
sarah-burton-s-givenchy-finds-its-voice-.dek: At the Chateau de Vincennes, the former McQueen designer stops negotiating with the past and starts conversing with it — power suits, Stephen Jones headpieces, and a rare standing ovation.

body.text: The show notes opened with a question: 'How can we put ourselves back together in the world we're living in?' Sarah Burton answered with clothes. Specifically, with formidable gentleman's tailoring worn by shorn-headed women in a stark white structure built inside the Chateau de Vincennes — double-breasted, pinstriped, with shirt and tie. It was exactly what Hubert de Givenchy himself wore every day of his career.

This was Burton's third collection for Givenchy, and the one where critics agree she found her voice. After two seasons of precise groundwork — silhouette studies, structural exercises, and a careful triangulation of Hubert de Givenchy, Lee Alexander McQueen, and her own instincts — this collection marks the moment when everything cohered.
The Power Suit, Reinvented

The power suits were the collection's spine. Sharp 'manly' silhouettes with double-breasted jackets and baggy pants stood alongside more feminine versions with cinched waists, peplums, and enormous bows front and back. Broad-shouldered trouser suits in classic men's fabrics, occasionally draped in a 'big boss coat,' were described by one critic as 'undeniably convincing.' Burton was not proposing one ideal woman but many women and many ways of being strong — armored and beautiful simultaneously.
The T-Shirt Secret

Legendary milliner Stephen Jones returned to Givenchy headquarters after 30 years to create the collection's headpieces, and they had a solemn, chaste grandeur — part Benedictine abbess, part 1960s Richard Avedon couture portrait, a couple of parts old Dutch master. But backstage, Jones revealed their humble origin: they were all made from T-shirts. Silk T-shirts, transformed into turbans in a clotted cream-like satin. The tension between grand appearance and modest material felt like a metaphor for the entire collection.

The Northern European Old Master references ran deep. Themes from Vermeer and Rembrandt surfaced in the lighting, the fabrics, the color palette — muted ivories, deep blacks, and sudden bursts of gold. Film icon Marlene Dietrich emerged subtly throughout, echoing the timeless balance between strength and glamour that Burton was chasing.
Personal Archaeology

The most moving moments came when Burton reached into her own past. Leopard prints — long favored by both Givenchy and Alexander McQueen — included the exact black leopard lace that McQueen himself used in one of his Fall collections, a personal excavation from her 28 years at the house. A yellow jacquard was excavated from one of McQueen's own Givenchy collections, woven into new pieces with the reverence of a relic and the confidence of a woman who has earned the right to use it.

Furry coats, a Hollywood glam bustier gown, and a sequin-fringed mini dress in animal print brought a wilder edge to the highly studied lineup. The materials — velvet, kimono silks, lace, silver bullion — were chosen with the specificity of someone who has spent three decades thinking about cloth.
An Unexpected Guest

In a delightful surprise, Emily Green — guitarist of the Brooklyn band Geese — made her runway debut walking in the show. Later that night, Geese played a gig at Paris's Le Bataclan. The casting felt right: youth and art and music woven into the fabric of the collection itself.

The front row — Rooney Mara, Diane Kruger, Alexa Chung, Elizabeth Olsen, Yseult, and Emma Thynn — watched the show close to a rare standing ovation, one of the most emotionally charged moments of Paris Fashion Week this season. Burton is no longer negotiating with the past. She is conversing with it.

loewe-s-mccollough-hernandez-push-materi.title: Loewe's McCollough & Hernandez Push Material Innovation to Its Limits
loewe-s-mccollough-hernandez-push-materi.author: Willie Austin
loewe-s-mccollough-hernandez-push-materi.dek: At the Chateau de Vincennes, the former Proenza Schouler duo stages 65 looks that ask whether luxury can also be fun — with 3D-printed latex, inflatable parkas, and a menagerie of blue velvet sea creatures.

body.text: Inside the Chateau de Vincennes, a medieval castle in eastern Paris, Loewe had constructed something unexpected: a huge structure covered in green gingham print, with lacquered yellow floors creating a runway that looked like it belonged in a children's picture book. Giant blue velvet sea creatures — octopi, hermit crabs, orcas, lobsters, whales — perched on oversized shoeboxes throughout the venue, sitting among the audience as if they were fellow guests. The sculptures were the work of Cologne-based artist Cosima von Bonin, whose past exhibitions bear titles like 'Who's Exploiting Who in the Deep Sea?' It was, by any measure, the most joyful set of Paris Fashion Week.

Jack McCollough and Lazaro Hernandez — the duo behind Proenza Schouler for over two decades — met as students at Parsons School of Design, where their senior thesis collection was bought in its entirety by Barneys New York. They named their brand after their mothers' maiden names. Now, in their second season at Loewe, they are asking a question that few luxury houses dare to pose: can high fashion also be fun?
Engineering Impossibilities

The 65-look collection was an engineering exhibition disguised as a fashion show. Slip dresses and pyjama tops appeared as 3D-printed replicas cast in latex — viscous, slick surfaces that caught the light with every step, mimicking the flow of fabric while remaining strangely frozen in time. The show opened with a glossy, perfectly folded 'lace' dress that exemplified this technique.

Parkas and scarves were inflated to whale-size proportions using a specialized industrial process of laser-cutting, bonding, and sealing to create airtight seams. Leather macs featured inflatable pocket flaps, hoods, and inner channels. Bouclé coats were made of looped lacquered leather. Tartan sweaters and dresses were knitted using incredibly thin leather yarn — a material innovation that sounds impossible until you see it moving on a body.

Then there were the climbing rope dresses: dangling lengths of thick spools of climbing rope looped into party dresses, beaded rope transformed into eveningwear. Shift dresses seemingly made from tartan laundry bags. Their signature snap-on Polly Pocket jackets returned in brown and orange. The palette screamed in lurid yellows and highlighter greens.
Innovation as Luxury

The duo's message was unmistakable: innovation is the new luxury. In a season where most houses were retreating into pragmatism and safe tailoring, McCollough and Hernandez went the opposite direction — pushing materials to their breaking point, then pushing further. Each piece was an engineering feat that could only exist because of Loewe's extraordinary atelier and its century-old expertise in leather craft.

Dazed called it 'joyous, young and zesty,' noting a fresh approach to luxury that is 'unserious, playful and even weird at times.' 10 Magazine wrote: 'Punchy, playful and zinged with colour — the Loewe boys are bringing real joy to serious luxury.' Not everyone was convinced — nss magazine suggested the execution was 'rather mediocre' — but the ambition was undeniable.

Models walked to a bassy techno soundtrack past von Bonin's blue velvet menagerie, and for a few minutes, the grind of fashion month — the fatigue, the cynicism, the endless black — dissolved into something approaching wonder. Whether that wonder translates to sales remains the question. But as a statement of intent, it was electrifying.

celine-s-michael-rider-designs-for-the-e.title: Celine's Michael Rider Designs for the Everyday Parisian
celine-s-michael-rider-designs-for-the-e.author: Willie Austin
celine-s-michael-rider-designs-for-the-e.dek: The former Phoebe Philo protege delivers what WWD calls 'probably the best wardrobe so far in the international season' — slim tailoring, quiet confidence, and clothes that actually work.

body.text: Inside a giant wooden box tucked in a courtyard behind the Institut de France on the Left Bank, fitted with imposing wood and steel speakers, Michael Rider showed his third collection for Celine. It was not loud. It was not conceptual. It was not trying to go viral. It was, according to WWD, 'probably the best wardrobe so far in the international season' — and the highest compliment a fashion critic can pay a designer who believes in clothes over concepts.

Rider's path to the creative director's chair is unusual. Raised in Washington, D.C., he attended Brown University before being selected by Nicolas Ghesquiere as his 'pin giver' at Balenciaga — a role where he stood by the designer's side during fittings outfitted with two holsters filled with pins, scissors, and ribbons. He spent four years there before Phoebe Philo recruited him as head designer when she was named creative director of Celine in 2008. That decade under Philo shaped everything.
Classics With Bite

Rider describes his approach as 'classics with bite' and 'character over costume.' He favors intuition over strategy. The collection bore this out: ankle-length trenches cut through with flashes of silk color, a khaki trench buttoned only at the top to reveal a silver dress beneath, sharply cropped blazers over slim-cut trousers, and swags of glossy white satin that served as evening alternatives.

The white satin pieces — simple tunics demarcated with bows — echoed Philo's influential Spring 2013 collection, 'though buffed to a much higher polish,' as one critic noted. Rider is not replicating the Philo era. He is metabolizing it, filtering it through his years at Ralph Lauren (where he served as creative director from 2018 to 2024), and arriving at something quieter, sharper, and entirely his own.
Slim Tailoring Returns

After years of oversized dressing dominating runways, Rider concentrated on slimmer silhouettes because, he said, it 'just felt fresh.' Slim tailoring is back at the center of fashion, and Celine made the most convincing case for it. The collection moved effortlessly from morning to evening: a camel coat for the Metro, a slip dress for dinner, a leather jacket that works for both. Demonstrative derby and bucket hats added personality. Squishy plimsolls and kitten-heel granny boots, often in white — 'outre for fall,' one reviewer noted — grounded the looks in reality.

Where Hedi Slimane's Celine (2018-2024) brought rock-and-roll drama and youth-oriented edge, Rider brings restrained sophistication with unexpected disruptions. The collection's genius was in its specificity: each look felt like it belonged to a particular woman on a particular day, not to a mood board or a marketing strategy.

Marie Claire called it a 'breakout' show — 'not for trend followers, it's for people with bite.' The Impression declared that 'Celine under Michael Rider is a house built on precision.' Some critics noted the aesthetic veers close to territory occupied by The Row, but Rider's particular thrill lies in 'rendering something familiar slightly strange' — a collar that sits wrong, a hem that breaks unexpectedly, a flash of silver where you expected none.

It was, in the end, a collection that emulated those Celine advertising campaigns plastering bus stops around Paris — slightly snobby but terribly attractive gilded youths dressed in idealized visions of what cool Parisians may be wearing tomorrow. Rider's Celine woman doesn't try hard. She just is.

herm-s-conjures-a-liminal-realm-between-.title: Hermes Conjures a Liminal Realm Between Day and Evening, Earth and Space
herm-s-conjures-a-liminal-realm-between-.author: Willie Austin
herm-s-conjures-a-liminal-realm-between-.dek: Nadege Vanhee titles her collection after the French expression for dusk — the moment when you cannot tell a dog from a wolf — and delivers clothes built to last a lifetime.

body.text: The barracks of the Garde Republicaine — the French army division that protects the president and government — had been transformed into something primeval. Moss carpeted the floor. Green undergrowth crept along the walls. Above it all, a recreated sky in deep, bruised blue suggested the last minutes before nightfall. Nadege Vanhee titled her Autumn/Winter 2026 collection 'Entre chien et loup' — the French expression for twilight, the liminal moment when you cannot tell a dog from a wolf, when the familiar becomes unfamiliar and strange.

Vanhee, who was born in Seclin, France to a French father and Algerian mother, studied at the Royal Academy of Fine Arts in Antwerp before stints at Maison Martin Margiela, Celine under Phoebe Philo, and The Row in New York. She joined Hermes in 2014, becoming the first woman to lead the house's women's ready-to-wear in over 20 years. After a decade of quiet, steady work, her title was elevated in 2024, and her collections have become appointment viewing.
The Zip as Metaphor

Zips were the collection's central motif — slicing diagonally across jackets, running the full length of dresses, spiraling on biker dresses to reveal controlled glimpses of skin. They were functional but also decorative, suggesting that every garment could be opened up, reconfigured, made new. In Vanhee's hands, the zip became a metaphor for the collection itself: a certain way of talking about sexiness through revealing and concealing.

The house's iconic silk carres — featuring the 1951 'Perspective' design by A.M. Cassandre — were draped like capes or loosely tied across the shoulders, their geometric lines framing the body. They also appeared on quilted gilets, bomber jackets, and shirt coats. A silk scarf worn as a cape was the season's most photographed moment — proof that at Hermes, the simplest gesture carries the most weight.
Craft Beyond Measure

The craftsmanship was staggering even by Hermes standards. Glossy lambskin cycling shorts were paired with aviator jackets — modified jodhpurs that tied the collection to the house's equestrian heritage. Shearling bombers in dark blues and deep russets had the density and warmth of objects built to outlast their owner. Shimmering silk twill moved like liquid metal under the twilight lighting.

Vanhee described dressing strong women who can 'take control, walk in the night and chart new territory.' The AP wire called the collection 'a deliberate shift into something more enigmatic, moody, and quietly compelling.' AnOther Magazine praised its 'strong female agency.' In a season dominated by pragmatism and commercial anxiety, Hermes offered something rarer: clothes that feel like they exist outside of time, objects you keep forever.

That is the Hermes proposition, of course — the idea that a garment or an object can transcend fashion's relentless churn of seasons and trends. Vanhee understands this better than almost anyone working today. Her collection didn't shout. It didn't need to. It simply stood in the twilight and waited for you to come closer.

jean-paul-gaultier-by-duran-lantink-chan.title: Jean Paul Gaultier by Duran Lantink Channels Marlene Dietrich
jean-paul-gaultier-by-duran-lantink-chan.author: Willie Austin
jean-paul-gaultier-by-duran-lantink-chan.dek: The Dutch upcycling prodigy — who once designed Janelle Monae's vagina trousers — delivers his vindication season, earning a standing ovation and three 'Fabulous!' shouts from Jean Paul himself.

body.text: The starting point was a vintage mesh T-shirt from Duran Lantink's own wardrobe, featuring Marlene Dietrich. From that single image — Dietrich in a man's suit, cigarette in hand, gender dissolved in smoke — Lantink built 44 looks that channeled the actress's feminine-masculinity into something entirely contemporary. 'I wanted to make smoking dresses,' he said. 'Cinematic characters.' A column dress featured Dietrich smoking, with billowing smoke effects rendered in fabric. It was fashion as portraiture, cloth as biography.

Lantink, 37, was born in The Hague, Netherlands, and studied at the Gerrit Rietveld Academie before graduating from the Sandberg Instituut in Amsterdam in 2017. He became famous for his 'collage' technique — splicing discarded luxury garments from Givenchy, Louis Vuitton, and Gucci into new hybrid pieces. In 2018, he designed the 'vagina trousers' for Janelle Monae's 'Pynk' music video. His client list reads like a festival lineup: Beyonce, Billie Eilish, Lizzo, Doja Cat.
From Guest to Heir

After Jean Paul Gaultier's retirement from the runway in January 2020, the house introduced a rotating guest designer model for its couture line — Chitose Abe, Glenn Martens, Olivier Rousteing, Haider Ackermann, Julien Dossena, Simone Rocha, Ludovic de Saint Sernin, and Nicolas Di Felice all took turns. In April 2025, the rotation ended when Lantink was named permanent creative director, the first sole successor to Gaultier. He also revived the ready-to-wear line, dormant since 2014.

His debut (Spring/Summer 2026) had divided opinion. 'While some applauded his want for disruption, many decried his apparent affront to good taste,' Dazed reported. Lantink acknowledged the criticism: 'I knew that criticism would be coming, but not necessarily in the way that it happened.' This sophomore collection was his answer.
Pinstripes and Delirium

The show opened with simple tailoring — a black suit underneath a long pleated overcoat — which was then systematically deconstructed over 44 looks. By look four, pinstripes had arrived with the shirt collar yanked up and repurposed as a hood. W Magazine documented the escalation: 'slim-cut pinstripe trousers with pinstripe overlain underwear and pinstripe stirrups holding pinstripe pant legs.' Sculptural tailoring grew out of archive pinstripe suits from past couture collections, morphing into something neither Gaultier nor Lantink could have made alone.

The hat brims were the collection's most talked-about detail. The back brim of a cowboy hat jutted out and curled down to attach to the waistcoat beneath. Another had a hood extending from the back, giving it an otherworldly quality — part Western, part medieval, part something that doesn't have a name yet. Pleated black gowns and Fair Isle knit bodysuits filled out the lineup with vintage glamour rebuilt for 2026.

The finale — a sequence of not-so-little black dresses — drew a standing ovation. Backstage, Jean Paul Gaultier himself offered his verdict: 'Fabulous! Fabulous! Fabulous!' 10 Magazine called it 'some of the most exciting silhouettes of Paris Fashion Week.' The prodigal upcycler has earned his throne.

alexander-mcqueen-se-n-mcgirr-s-romantic.title: Alexander McQueen: Sean McGirr's Romantic Darkness
alexander-mcqueen-se-n-mcgirr-s-romantic.author: Willie Austin
alexander-mcqueen-se-n-mcgirr-s-romantic.dek: In his fifth collection for the house, the Irish designer finds a measure of soul — frozen flowers, chain-mail knitwear, and a Todd Haynes-inspired set of sheer curtains and voyeurism.

body.text: Broadway theatre designer Tom Scutt built a spare, white labyrinthine space sectioned off by gauzy curtains in a peachy tone, creating three separate viewing areas. Models walked through the maze to a central platform, visible and then invisible, watched and then hidden. Sean McGirr said the curtains were meant to 'convey voyeurism and a weird sort of eroticism.' The concept drew from Todd Haynes's 1995 film 'Safe' — about surveillance, paranoia, and performance. Once the final model took her position, the curtains lifted so all 39 looks could be seen together. It was a reveal that felt earned.

McGirr, born in Dublin in 1988, studied at Central Saint Martins before stints at Dries Van Noten, Uniqlo, and Burberry. He joined JW Anderson in 2020 as head of menswear, eventually becoming director of ready-to-wear. He was named creative director of Alexander McQueen in October 2023, succeeding Sarah Burton after her 13-year tenure. This was his fifth collection for the house — and, critics largely agree, his strongest.
Flowers in Ice

The floral motif that ran through the collection had an origin story that could only happen at McQueen. 'We got all these flowers, trapped them in blocks of ice, and we started smashing them and then we took photos,' McGirr explained. The results — fractured, violent, beautiful — were translated into three-dimensional floral wallpaper textures that peeked through glossy satin ensembles, shrunken tailored suits, and bloomer short sets. Floral patterns typically found on domestic wallpaper were liberated, becoming rose-quilted bombers and eveningwear in Hitchcockian hues.

The final gown evoked, in McGirr's words, 'the freedom of going out to a garden of fresh flowers' — a release after the claustrophobia of the curtained maze. It was one part English garden, one part haunted manor, and entirely McQueen.
Chain-Mail and Lace

Chain-mail armor was reimagined as knitwear — heavy metallic mesh softened into poplin shirts and cable-knit jumpers. Delicate lace typically reserved for the bedroom became dramatic eveningwear. Porcelain doll masks with visible cracks sat on several models' faces, adding a layer of psychological unease that channeled the house's founder without mimicking him.

McGirr's McQueen is not Lee McQueen's — it lacks the theatrical shock and conceptual fury of the founder. Nor is it Sarah Burton's romantic fairy-tale femininity. It is something more psychological and referential, filtering specific McQueen collections (La Poupee SS97, Dante AW96, It's Only a Game SS05) through contemporary anxieties about digital life, surveillance, and performance.
A Divided Verdict

Critical reception remained mixed. Business of Fashion critic Angelo Flaccavento was blunt: 'Today's McQueen is mere clothing,' suggesting a disconnect from the founder's 'conceptual rigor and worldview communication through fashion language.' The Impression was more generous, scoring it 8.6 out of 10 and calling it 'a welcome turning point' — McGirr 'saying screw it, this is me.' W Magazine credited him with finding 'a measure of soul' while delivering commercially viable tailoring and eveningwear.

The soundtrack — an original score by Grammy-winning artist A.G. Cook featuring foreboding electronic synths — underscored the tension between beauty and menace that McGirr is slowly making his own. Five collections in, the question is no longer whether he can design. It's whether he can make you feel something. On this evidence, he's getting closer.

stars-hit-paris-runways-but-fall-s-real-.title: Stars Hit Paris Runways, But Fall's Real Trend Was Dressing for Hard Times
stars-hit-paris-runways-but-fall-s-real-.author: Willie Austin
stars-hit-paris-runways-but-fall-s-real-.dek: With 46% of executives expecting conditions to worsen and tariffs reshaping the global supply chain, Paris Fashion Week's Fall 2026 season marked a decisive pivot toward pragmatism.

body.text: Paris Fashion Week Women's ran March 2-10, 2026, and the question hovering over every show was the same: how do you dress people when the world feels dark, loud, and unstable? The AP's overarching verdict was that the biggest houses are in 'reset mode,' and for the first time in years, the strongest shows were not the ones with the most dramatic sets or the most famous front rows. They were the ones that made you think: I would actually wear that.

Three trends emerged across the week. First, clothes built to shield — high collars, wrapped coats, strong tailoring, silhouettes that looked guarded and protective. Second, a sharper line — after years of oversized dressing, designers moved back toward shape and definition. Third, rougher glamour — messier hair, smudged makeup, darker moods. Luxury no longer looked sealed off from real life.
The Numbers Behind the Mood

The mood was not accidental. According to the Business of Fashion and McKinsey's annual State of Fashion report, 46 percent of fashion executives expect industry conditions to worsen in 2026 — up eight percentage points from last year. Seventy-six percent say tariffs will be the biggest issue defining the year. The weighted average of tariffs on apparel and footwear imports to the United States spiked from 13 percent to 54 percent in spring 2025 before easing to 36 percent — still well above historical norms. Fifty-five percent of executives expect further price increases in 2026.

Meanwhile, the luxury customer base has shrunk from 400 million people in 2022 to roughly 340 million in 2025, according to Bain & Company. The industry is projected to lose another 20 to 30 million clients. When your customer base is contracting and your costs are rising, the most honest response is to make clothes that earn their place in people's lives. That is exactly what happened.
House by House

Celine's Michael Rider delivered refined essentials with bolder flourishes — silk lending the collection a fluid line, slim tailoring pushed back to the center. Givenchy's Sarah Burton answered with power suits and sculptural velvet. Balenciaga's Pierpaolo Piccioli built his entire show around darkness and the search for light, collaborating with Euphoria creator Sam Levinson. Vivienne Westwood's Andreas Kronthaler staged grief, eroticism, and disorder simultaneously — rough seams, smudged lipstick, lingerie codes, an unfinished bride.

Dior's Jonathan Anderson opened the week with his sophomore womenswear show. Chanel's Matthieu Blazy presented his second act. Coperni did not show at all, citing 'a significant deterioration in their relationship with a minority shareholder' and framing the decision as a structural reset — perhaps the most brutally honest statement of the season.
The Celebrity Factor

The front rows were stacked: Oprah Winfrey appeared at four shows in the opening stretch, Chappell Roan turned up at Vivienne Westwood in a dramatic hairdo before heading to McQueen, and the usual constellation of actors and musicians orbited every venue. But the AP's conclusion cut through the noise: 'The strongest shows suggested a week less interested in escape than in resilience. The best designers were not trying to make the world disappear. They were trying to arm women for it.'

Fashion has always reflected its moment. In times of prosperity, it expands — louder colors, bigger silhouettes, more embellishment. In times of anxiety, it contracts — sharper lines, darker palettes, clothes that function as armor. Fall 2026 was unmistakably a season of contraction. But within that contraction, there was also clarity. And clarity, in fashion, is its own kind of luxury.

luxury-fashion-rethinks-its-value-after-.title: Luxury Fashion Rethinks Its Value After Years of Price Hikes
luxury-fashion-rethinks-its-value-after-.author: Willie Austin
luxury-fashion-rethinks-its-value-after-.dek: A Chanel bag that cost $4,900 in 2016 now retails for $11,300. The industry lost 60 million customers in three years. Something has to give.

body.text: Here is a number that tells the story of luxury fashion's last decade: the Chanel Medium Classic Flap Bag cost $4,900 in 2016. By 2019, it was $5,800. By the end of 2021, it had jumped to $8,800 — a 35 percent increase in a single year. In April 2023, it crossed $10,000. As of August 2025, it retails for $11,300. That is a 130 percent increase in nine years, with at least one price hike per year since 2016. The bag has not changed. The leather has not improved. The craftsmanship has not evolved. Only the number on the tag has moved.

The tenth annual State of Fashion report, co-produced by Business of Fashion and McKinsey & Company and subtitled 'When the Rules Change,' identifies this as the central crisis facing the luxury industry in 2026. After years of price-led growth — raising prices as a substitute for innovation — brands are being forced to confront an uncomfortable truth: consumers have noticed, and they are leaving.
The Shrinking Customer

According to a Bain & Company and Altagamma study published in November 2025, the luxury customer base shrank from 400 million people in 2022 to approximately 340 million in 2025. The industry is projected to lose another 20 to 30 million clients. Bain partner Federica Levato used a striking word: even ultra-wealthy clients feel 'betrayed' by the pricing.

The betrayal is specific. When a brand raises prices 10 to 15 percent annually without a corresponding improvement in materials, design, or experience, the implicit contract between maker and buyer is broken. The customer is no longer paying for quality or creativity. They are paying for the privilege of the logo. And as the BoF report documents, that privilege is losing its power.
The Hollowing Out

The consequences extend far beyond the luxury tier. Value brands like Bershka and H&M have reduced the share of SKUs in their lowest price tiers in the UK by 15 to 25 percent between 2023 and 2025. The entire industry is racing upmarket as the middle hollows out. Mid-market brands have overtaken luxury as fashion's 'main value creator' in 2024, according to the report — the fastest-growing segment, precisely because they are offering the quality and design that luxury once monopolized, at prices that feel proportionate.

The irony is rich: luxury raised prices to signal exclusivity, but in doing so, they created space for competitors to offer the same emotional and material satisfaction at a fraction of the cost. Seventy-eight percent of industry leaders now view declining consumer confidence as the sector's most significant challenge.
The Tariff Trap

Compounding the pricing crisis is the tariff shock. US apparel prices rose 1.3 percent between January and July 2025. The weighted average of tariffs on apparel and footwear imports spiked to 54 percent before settling at 36 percent — still nearly triple pre-2025 levels. Seventy-six percent of executives say tariffs will define 2026, and the most common mitigation strategy cited in the report is, predictably, raising prices. It is a vicious cycle: tariffs increase costs, brands pass costs to consumers, consumers leave, brands lose volume, brands raise prices again to compensate.

Kering CEO Luca de Meo reportedly told employees in an internal memo that the group must rethink its pricing and product strategy — a rare acknowledgment from inside a major luxury house that the model is broken. Yet 31 percent of shoppers still admit willingness to 'splurge on fashion if it is the right product.' The demand hasn't disappeared. It has become conditional. Consumers want to know what they're paying for, and 'because we can charge this much' is no longer a sufficient answer.
The era of price-led growth is over. What comes next will require something the luxury industry has been surprisingly reluctant to offer: actual value for the price.

burberry-celebrates-170-years-with-iconi.title: Burberry Celebrates 170 Years With Iconic Trench Portraits
burberry-celebrates-170-years-with-iconi.author: Willie Austin
burberry-celebrates-170-years-with-iconi.dek: Tim Walker photographs 23 cultural figures in black and white for 'The Trench, Portraits of an Icon' — a campaign that doubles as a thesis statement for Joshua Schulman's turnaround.

body.text: In 1856, a 21-year-old former draper's apprentice named Thomas Burberry opened a small outfitter's shop in Basingstoke, Hampshire. Twenty-three years later, he invented gabardine — a hardwearing, water-resistant yet breathable fabric where the yarn is waterproofed before weaving. During World War I, British officers adapted gabardine coats for the trenches, and the trench coat was born. One hundred and seventy years later, the garment remains the most recognizable single piece in fashion.

To mark the anniversary, Burberry commissioned photographer Tim Walker to shoot 'The Trench, Portraits of an Icon' — a series of intimate black-and-white portraits that feel, in the brand's words, 'immediate and timeless, with an easy sophistication.' The format is deliberately stripped back: no sets, no locations, no distractions. Just the trench and the person inside it, a collar turned up, a belt loosely tied, the garment at once protective and expressive.
23 Faces

The cast spans film, music, sport, and fashion: Kate Moss, Kendall Jenner, Rosie Huntington-Whiteley, Daisy Edgar-Jones, Jonathan Bailey, Matthew Macfadyen, Kristin Scott Thomas, Kid Cudi, Little Simz, Teyana Taylor, Karen Elson, Erin O'Connor, Agyness Deyn, Jack Draper, Eberechi Eze, Hikaru Utada, J.Y. Park, Wu Lei, Bright, Reece Clarke of the Royal Ballet, Alva Claire, Iris Lasnet, and Sora Choi. An accompanying film captures candid, unscripted exchanges between cast and crew, soundtracked by 'Sing' by Blur.

The breadth of the cast is the point. A Crystal Palace footballer, a Japanese singer-songwriter, a K-pop mogul, a Royal Ballet dancer, a Thai actor — the trench coat is presented not as a luxury item but as a cultural constant, something that means something different to everyone who wears it.
Schulman's Bet

The campaign is inseparable from CEO Joshua Schulman's turnaround strategy. Appointed in July 2024, Schulman diagnosed the problem bluntly: 'We took pricing too high across the board.' Previous strategies had tried to transform Burberry into a top-end, fashion-forward leather goods house — 'square peg, round hole,' he called them. His plan, dubbed 'Burberry Forward,' centers on leading with outerwear, then 'earning the right' in other categories.

The numbers suggest it's working. Shares surged 70 percent under Schulman, and Burberry reentered the FTSE 100 in September 2025 with a 4.6 billion pound valuation. Adjusted operating profit swung from a 41 million pound loss to 19 million in profit. Gross margin climbed to 67.9 percent. Inventory was reduced by 24 percent. Christmas 2025 sales beat forecasts. Shares are up 27 percent in 2026, outperforming LVMH, Hermes, Kering, and Ferragamo.
Made in England

Each Heritage Collection coat and jacket is made at Burberry's Castleford manufacturing site, where specialist tailors have produced the house's rainwear for more than 50 years. All fabrics — from the shower-resistant outer shell to the cotton lining with the iconic House Check — are woven at the Burberry Mill in Keighley, Yorkshire. The main material and body lining contain 100 percent organic cotton. In an industry increasingly defined by offshore production and opaque supply chains, the trench coat's provenance is part of its proposition.

Pop-up installations launched at Regent Street in London, Isetan Shinjuku in Tokyo, Lotte Main in Seoul, and 57th Street in New York. The campaign signals a year-long celebration of the house's archive and heritage — and a bet that in a fractured, uncertain fashion landscape, the most powerful thing a brand can do is remember what it's for.

saks-global-files-for-bankruptcy-owing-b.title: Saks Global Files for Bankruptcy Owing Billions to Fashion's Biggest Names
saks-global-files-for-bankruptcy-owing-b.author: Willie Austin
saks-global-files-for-bankruptcy-owing-b.dek: The parent of Saks Fifth Avenue, Neiman Marcus, and Bergdorf Goodman files Chapter 11 after a $2.7 billion acquisition saddled it with unsustainable debt — and Chanel alone is owed $136 million.

body.text: On December 31, 2025, Saks Global skipped a $100 million debt payment to its bondholders. Two weeks later, on January 13, 2026, the company and 112 affiliated entities filed voluntary petitions for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas. The filing listed estimated assets and liabilities both in the range of $1 billion to $10 billion, and a creditor count between 10,001 and 25,000. It is the most significant luxury retail collapse in a generation.

The bankruptcy was triggered by Saks Global's $2.7 billion acquisition of Neiman Marcus Group, finalized in December 2024. The deal — engineered by Hudson's Bay Company executive chairman Richard Baker — was supposed to create a luxury retail empire by merging Saks Fifth Avenue, its off-price chain Saks Off 5th, Neiman Marcus, and Bergdorf Goodman under one corporate umbrella. Instead, it saddled the company with unsustainable debt that it could not service.
Who's Owed What

The list of unsecured creditors reads like a who's who of global luxury. Chanel is owed $136 million, making it the largest unsecured creditor. Kering — parent of Gucci, Saint Laurent, and Balenciaga — is owed nearly $60 million. LVMH is owed $26 million. Beiersdorf owes $22.2 million, Estee Lauder $16 million, and Puig $12.1 million. Hundreds of smaller brands face the prospect of never recovering what they are owed.

The filing sent shockwaves through the fashion industry not because luxury retail is struggling — that has been obvious for years — but because of the sheer scale of the debt and the speed of the collapse. Neiman Marcus itself had filed for Chapter 11 in May 2020 during the pandemic. The fact that it was acquired, folded into a larger entity, and dragged back into bankruptcy within two years speaks to the recklessness of the deal.
Leadership in Freefall

The leadership turmoil was equally dramatic. Marc Metrick, a Saks executive for approximately three decades, departed as CEO in early January. Richard Baker, who had engineered the Neiman acquisition, was appointed CEO on January 2 — only to leave the company entirely two weeks later. Geoffroy van Raemdonck, who had led Neiman Marcus through its own 2020 bankruptcy, was appointed CEO upon the Chapter 11 filing on January 14.
Store Closures

Since the filing, Saks Global has announced the closure of 24 department stores in two waves. The first wave shuttered eight Saks Fifth Avenue stores and one Neiman Marcus location (Boston's Copley Place) by end of April. The second wave, announced March 6, closed 12 more Saks stores and three additional Neiman Marcus locations — Canyon Park, California; Honolulu, Hawaii; and White Plains, New York — by end of May. All but 12 Saks Off 5th locations are being shuttered.

Post-restructuring, the company plans to operate 13 Saks Fifth Avenue stores (including the Manhattan Fifth Avenue flagship), 32 Neiman Marcus locations, and Bergdorf Goodman in New York. The company secured $1.75 billion in committed capital, including $1 billion in debtor-in-possession financing for operations during bankruptcy and $500 million available upon emergence. Nearly $600 million was earmarked for catch-up payments to vendors.

The broader context is grim. Declining foot traffic, rising online competition, and the unsustainable debt loads created by private-equity-style acquisitions have hollowed out American luxury retail. The Saks bankruptcy is not an isolated event. It is the logical endpoint of an era in which financial engineering replaced merchant skill, and the customer — the actual person walking through the door — became an afterthought.

demna-s-primavera-gucci-debut-divides-cr.title: Demna's Primavera Gucci Debut Divides Critics
demna-s-primavera-gucci-debut-divides-cr.author: Willie Austin
demna-s-primavera-gucci-debut-divides-cr.dek: The former Balenciaga provocateur stands before Botticelli's 'Primavera' and decides to make Gucci sexy again — with seamless construction, Kate Moss in a diamond-studded GG thong, and a critical establishment that cannot agree on what it just saw.

body.text: Demna stood before Sandro Botticelli's 'Primavera' and 'The Birth of Venus' in Florence, absorbing proportion, desire, and what he called 'the emotional architecture of beauty.' Then he went back to the studio and made clothes that split the fashion world in half. His first runway collection for Gucci — titled 'Primavera,' after the painting that also inspired the house's Flora fragrance — debuted on February 27, 2026, on a marble-clad set evocative of a museum. In a pre-show open letter, he described this Gucci as 'lighter, softer, more refined.' It was also, depending on who you ask, either genius or cynicism.

Demna Gvasalia was born on March 25, 1981, in Georgia. He studied international economics at Tbilisi State University before graduating with a master's in Fashion Design from the Royal Academy of Fine Arts in Antwerp in 2006. He worked at Maison Martin Margiela, served as senior designer of women's ready-to-wear at Louis Vuitton, co-founded Vetements in 2014 with his brother Gurum, and was appointed creative director of Balenciaga in 2015, where he spent approximately a decade stretching the boundaries of what counts as luxury.
Why Gucci Needed a Reset

He arrived at a house in crisis. His predecessor Sabato De Sarno, who attempted a quiet-luxury approach, exited after two years that failed to reverse declining sales. Gucci revenues fell 24 percent in Q4 2024, 23 percent for the full year, and a further 25 percent in Q1 2025. Parent company Kering reported a 14 percent drop in first-quarter 2025 revenues to 3.88 billion euros. The brand that once defined maximalism under Alessandro Michele had lost its cultural charge.
The Body as Structure

Demna's answer was the body itself. Seamless construction and heat-sealed edges enhanced fit, with fabrics engineered to follow the body without restriction. Skintight black slacks, thin logo belts, sheer white minidresses in hosiery fabric — the collection was hedonistic, body-first, full of everyday co-ed basics with a sensual twist. It was a direct callback to the Tom Ford era (1994-2004), which defined 'sexy minimalism' at Gucci, and stood in stark contrast to Michele's busy, 1970s-inflected maximalism.

The seamless construction was genuinely innovative, requiring entirely new pattern-making techniques that eliminated traditional seams and darts. Whether this represented a meaningful advance in garment engineering or a gimmick dressed up as innovation was one of many points critics could not agree on.
Kate Moss and the Thong

Kate Moss closed the show in a glimmering backless gown paired with a white gold Gucci GG logo thong bedazzled with 10-carat diamonds — a direct callback to Ford's infamous 1998 piece. Karlie Kloss, Elsa Hosk, and Emily Ratajkowski made rare runway returns. The casting was calculated to project exactly the kind of cultural heat that Gucci had lost.

Supporters hailed it as the return of sexy at Gucci, a culturally charged debut that made 'Gucci an adjective again.' Critics saw undifferentiated basics riding the 'mob wife' trend with hints of fast fashion, and a cynical mining of the archive to recreate what was already selling. Additional controversy arose from Gucci posting AI-generated promotional images ahead of the show, which drew backlash.

Kering deputy CEO Francesca Bellettini tried to manage expectations: 'Demna is going to build on the vision of the brand. He's going to bring desirability and fashionability, but it's a build-up, not a cancellation.' The intensity of debate itself was seen as a success — 'the fervour with which Demna's Gucci Primavera debut has been dissected and debated is akin to not many other debuts in recent seasons.' In a season of safe bets and quiet tailoring, at least someone had the nerve to start a fight.

maria-grazia-chiuri-makes-her-fendi-debu.title: Maria Grazia Chiuri Makes Her Fendi Debut With Less I, More Us
maria-grazia-chiuri-makes-her-fendi-debu.author: Willie Austin
maria-grazia-chiuri-makes-her-fendi-debu.dek: The Italian designer returns to the house where she began her career in 1989, presenting a co-ed collection dominated by black, fluid satin, and the words 'Less I, more us' written across the runway floor.

body.text: The words appeared on the runway floor before a single model emerged: 'Less I, more us.' It was a statement of creative philosophy, but also autobiography. Maria Grazia Chiuri first walked through Fendi's doors in 1989, a young accessories designer working alongside the five Fendi sisters in the house's Rome atelier. She stayed for a decade before the LVMH acquisition changed everything. Then came Valentino, where she served as co-creative director alongside Pierpaolo Piccioli, and Dior, where in 2016 she became the first woman to lead the house in its history. Now she has come home.

Her debut Fall/Winter 2026 collection for Fendi, presented during Milan Fashion Week on February 25, 2026, was a deliberate exercise in restraint. 'I wanted one wardrobe, not two,' she said, presenting menswear and womenswear together in a co-ed format that marked a departure from Fendi's traditional separate-season approach.
A Roman Homecoming

Rome is central to everything Chiuri makes. 'You can try things without too many expectations,' she has said. 'You don't feel under pressure in Rome. You can make mistakes and even fail and then begin again. From that aspect, Rome is really more welcoming.' After staging her final Dior show in Rome, she carried that Roman sensibility into Fendi — injecting what critics called 'a new seductive Latin look' into a house that had stalled badly in the past three years under British designer Kim Jones.
The Case for Black

Approximately 70 percent of the collection was in black. Seventeen looks were entirely monochrome. 'I am tired of looking at colors on Instagram,' Chiuri stated — a sentence that could serve as a manifesto for the entire season. The black was not oppressive; it was clarifying. Satin and sheer dresses cut for movement, layered with matching boy shirts and bralettes. Sheer lace gowns and silk slips skimmed the body with tactile directness. Relaxed, everyday separates introduced a note of pragmatic resolve.

Fur — historically central to Fendi's identity — appeared in neon yellow-green and mixed-fur patchwork, plus accents on bags, vests, and collars. Sculptural collar chokers, detachable and sharp, became the collection's signature accessory. The collection moved between sinuous, streamlined tailoring and romantic flourishes — layers of sheer tulle and lace, plunging V-necklines, and bias-cut silk.
Less I, More Us

The phrase on the runway floor was not just a slogan. It was a reference to the collective creativity Chiuri experienced working alongside the Fendi sisters in the 1990s — a model of collaboration that she wants to revive. She spoke of seeking a return to 'emotion and desire' and of collaborating with women artists on the collection. After the aggressive individualism that defined luxury fashion's creative director era — the cult of the singular genius — Chiuri is proposing something more communal.

Critics acknowledged she 'performed admirably.' The collection was praised for its clear messaging, wearable elegance, and successful blend of daily separates, upscale workwear, and red-carpet gowns. Some winced at the paucity of color, and the heavy reliance on black was noted as potentially limiting. But as a debut — a first statement from a designer returning to a house she knows intimately — it was confident, coherent, and unmistakably her own.
